ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic congee entry (FOODON_00002539). No specific oyster congee entry exists; closest_parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of oyster congee, approximately 450 g, containing rice porridge, 4–6 sashimi-grade oysters, and pork mince in a savoury broth.

nutrient_source.title: Congee with seafood and pork — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries: Oysters, raw (FDC ID 175172); Rice, white, cooked (FDC ID 169756); Pork, ground, raw (FDC ID 168351); and HPB Singapore nutrient reference for congee base
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy) and Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for oyster congee with pork mince. Values estimated by weighted composite: approximately 55% cooked white rice porridge base, 25% raw sashimi-grade oysters, 15% cooked pork mince, 5% broth/condiments. USDA FDC Foundation Foods used for oysters and pork; HPB Foodo congee base used for rice porridge macros. Sodium elevated to reflect broth and soy sauce seasoning typical of restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice congee/porridge (GI ~57–65 range for white rice congee)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for oyster congee with pork mince. GI of 57 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain rice congee/porridge (white rice base, GI range 57–65; mid-point 57 used as conservative estimate). The addition of oysters and pork mince lowers the effective GI slightly due to protein and fat content; GI 57 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate. GL per serving calculated as: (57 × 37.8 g carbohydrate per 450 g serving) / 100 = 21.5, rounded to 21.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from broth, soy sauce, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 450 g restaurant serving provides an estimated 1395 mg sodium, representing approximately 60–70% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-sodium broth and limit soy sauce additions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 57) from white rice congee base with partial mitigation from protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice congee base carries a medium GI of approximately 57 and a glycemic load of ~21.6 per bowl; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size, pair with additional protein, and prefer a smaller serving to manage postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from pork mince, offset by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from oysters
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Oysters provide cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids and zinc, but the high sodium content from restaurant broth and the saturated fat from pork mince warrant caution; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion frequency and request low-sodium preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oyster Congee with Pork Mince (Sashimi-Grade Oyster) is a premium Chinese-Singaporean rice porridge dish featuring Sakoshi oysters — a Japanese-origin sashimi-grade oyster variety prized for their clean brine, creamy texture, and delicate mineral flavour — cooked in a rich pork and seafood broth alongside minced pork and aromatic condiments including ginger, spring onion, sesame oil, and white pepper. The dish is served half-cooked, meaning the oysters retain their raw, silky texture and are not fully heat-treated, making it unsuitable for young children, pregnant women, immunocompromised individuals, and the elderly. The congee base is made from white rice slow-simmered until it breaks down into a smooth, thick porridge. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy, high-protein meal providing approximately 72 kcal per 100 g, with notable contributions of vitamin B12 (from oysters), iron, zinc, and selenium. The sodium content is elevated due to restaurant-style broth and seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 57 (medium) based on the white rice congee base, with a glycemic load of approximately 21.6 per standard 450 g bowl. This dish is not halal due to the inclusion of pork mince. It is a popular gourmet seafood soup offering in Singapore, particularly associated with Teochew and Cantonese congee traditions.
common_user_queries: Is oyster congee safe to eat half-cooked?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for oyster congee with pork mince as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods entries for raw oysters, cooked white rice, and ground pork, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore congee base values. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain rice congee. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and the variability inherent in restaurant preparation.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no single approved-source entry covers this exact dish. Sodium is elevated to reflect typical restaurant broth and soy sauce seasoning. 2. The dish is described as 'half-cooked' in the source listing, meaning oysters are not fully heat-treated; this is a food safety consideration documented in the health context. 3. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 (confirmed zero — no trans fats are expected in this minimally processed seafood and rice dish per USDA FDC reference data). 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.7) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.5) are estimated from USDA FDC oyster and pork mince fatty acid profiles. 5. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database rice congee entry; actual GI may be slightly lower due to protein and fat content of oysters and pork. GL per serving = (57 × 37.8 g carbohydrate per 450 g serving) / 100 = 21.5, reported as 21.6 after rounding. 6. Vitamin B12 is notably high (4.5 mcg per 100 g) reflecting the oyster content; oysters are among the richest dietary sources of B12. 7. The dish contains pork and is therefore not halal. Not suitable for children, pregnant women, or immunocompromised individuals due to half-cooked oyster preparation. 8. All -1 sentinel values indicate nutrients not reported or not calculable from the composite source approach used; specifically: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g have been estimated and populated; all remaining -1 fields (none remain at -1 in this record) were resolved. Note: trans_fat_g = 0.0 is confirmed zero per USDA FDC reference — no trans fats present in this food by composition.
